title: Part I: Little Hintock
paragraphs: The rambler who, for old association's sake, should trace the forsaken coach-road running inland from Sherton Abbas, and, in the season of its leafing, follow it for a distance of fifteen miles, would find himself at the end of that distance in the vicinity of a human dwelling.

Fewer had been there in recent years than in the days of the coaches. The road was grass-grown now in places, the finger-posts leaning; and of the few who used it, fewer still left any record of their passing. But it led, ultimately, to Little Hintock—a hamlet of a few dozen souls, buried so deep in the woods that the surrounding forest seemed always on the point of swallowing it whole.

Little Hintock was a place of ancient trees and narrow paths and the smell of leaf mold. It was a place where people lived very close to nature—not from philosophy but from necessity. The men worked the timber: planting, felling, barking. The women kept gardens and tended to the cows and chickens and tried to keep some light burning.

Giles Winterborne was one of the young men of Little Hintock. He was a timber merchant's assistant—or had been, before the timber merchant's recent death left his position uncertain. He was a fine-looking young man of twenty-eight, honest and strong, with the kind of straightforward goodness that is only possible in people who have never been corrupted by ambition or sophistication.

He was in love with Grace Melbury, who had gone away to school and come back speaking differently. She was the timber merchant's daughter—his former employer's daughter—and she had come back from school polished and accomplished and slightly disconnected from everything she had known. She could play the piano now and speak French and talk about things she had read in books. And she was still fond of Giles, as one is fond of a place from one's childhood, but she was not sure that fond was the same as what she felt for him before.

Her father, Melbury, wanted better for her than Giles. He was a restless, conscience-stricken man, haunted by an old injury he had done to Giles's father, which made him want to make it right by giving his daughter to the son. But he also wanted Grace to do well, and Giles—decent, honest, limited Giles—might not constitute doing well.
Then a doctor came to Little Hintock. His name was Fitzpiers.

title: Part II: Doctor Fitzpiers
paragraphs: Dr. Edred Fitzpiers came to Little Hintock to practice and to study. He was a man of good family—an educated, ambitious, restless man who had somehow found himself in a country practice and was making the best of it while waiting for something better to come along.

He was not unkind, exactly. He was not a villain. He was charming and intelligent and genuinely interested in ideas—philosophy, medicine, psychology. He read Spinoza at night. He dissected things in the morning. He was interested in Grace Melbury from the first moment he saw her because she was the most refined creature in Little Hintock, and refinement was what he missed.

He pursued her with the deliberate charm that he could deploy when he chose. He read to her. He walked with her in the woods. He talked to her about ideas that no one else in Little Hintock talked about. Grace, who had been educated for a world that had not quite opened to her, found him intoxicating.

She broke off the informal understanding with Giles. It was not cruel, exactly—there was no formal engagement to break. But she had allowed him to hope, and now she withdrew the hope, and Giles understood and said nothing, because he was Giles and that was what Giles did.
She married Fitzpiers. She had a good wedding in the autumn, and they set up their household, and for a little while things were as pleasant as they had appeared.
Then Grace began to understand what she had married.

Fitzpiers was not faithful. He was the kind of man for whom fidelity was a temporary state—genuine while it lasted, but temporary. There was a woman in the neighborhood named Felice Charmond, a wealthy widow who owned a large portion of the woodland, and Fitzpiers found her interesting. His interest was reciprocated.
Grace found out. She said nothing at first. She had married him and she was in Little Hintock and she did not see what there was to do.
She began to walk in the woods again, as she had done as a girl. She began to meet Giles.

title: Part III: The Forest
paragraphs: Giles received her without comment or reproach. He made no claims on her. He treated her with the same honest, patient steadiness that had always been his character, and she began to see what she had passed over.

She did not love him—not yet, or not exactly. But she felt something in his company that she felt nowhere else: a sense of belonging, of simplicity, of being known. He did not require her to be educated or polished. He required nothing at all. He just was there, in the woods, planting his trees and doing his work.
Fitzpiers' affair with Mrs. Charmond became open knowledge. He eventually left Little Hintock to follow her abroad. Grace was alone.
She took steps to obtain a divorce. Under the new law it might be possible—if the desertion could be proved, if the adultery could be proved. Her father retained a solicitor. They waited.

Meanwhile Grace took refuge for a few days in a cottage of Giles's on the edge of the woodland, during a crisis of family conflict. Giles himself, in one of those acts of absolute selflessness that only certain kinds of people are capable of, gave her the cottage and slept outside in a makeshift shelter—this in autumn, in the rain, with a fever beginning.
The fever worsened. He would not come inside—would not compromise her reputation by being under the same roof. He lay in his shelter in the rain and refused to call for help.
He died. He died of the fever, of the rain and cold and his own stubborn honor.
Grace found him in the morning.

She understood then, too late, what it was she had lost. Not the romance she had expected—not Fitzpiers and his Spinoza and his sophisticated conversation. What she had lost was something older and more fundamental. Steadiness. Fidelity. A man who would sleep in the rain rather than harm her reputation.
Fitzpiers came back. He was repentant—genuinely, she thought. He had returned from the continent changed, or at least changed enough. He asked her forgiveness.
She gave it. She had no particular reason to, except that she had no particular reason not to, and Little Hintock was a long way from anywhere, and life was long.
She went back to Fitzpiers and the marriage continued. Whether it was happiness—whether she found, in the end, what she had been looking for—that was not entirely clear.

The woods of Little Hintock continued to stand, as they had always stood, patient and indifferent to the small human dramas playing out at their edges. The trees were planted and felled and planted again. The timber trade went on.
Only Giles was gone, and the hollow in his grove where he had slept in the rain, which the grass covered over by the following spring.
